title: VI: Mary Quite Contrary
html:
On the opening day of the York Assizes, the Grand Jury brought in a true bill, against Gerald, Duke of Denver, for murder. Gerald, Duke of Denver, being accordingly produced in the court, the Judge affected to discoverпїњвАФwhat, indeed, every newspaper in the country had been announcing to the world for the last fortnightпїњвАФthat he, being but a common or garden judge with a plebeian jury, was incompetent to try a peer of the realm. He added, however, that he would make it his business to inform the Lord Chancellor (who also, for the last fortnight, had been secretly calculating the accommodation in the Royal Gallery and choosing lords to form the Select Committee). Order being taken accordingly, the noble prisoner was led away.

A day or two later, in the gloom of a London afternoon, Mr.¬†Charles Parker rang the bell of a second-floor flat at No.¬†110 Piccadilly. The door was opened by Bunter, who informed him with a gracious smile that Lord Peter had stepped out for a few minutes but was expecting him, and would he kindly come in and wait.

вАЬWe only came up this morning,вАЭ added the valet, вАЬand are not quite straight yet, sir, if you will excuse us. Would you feel inclined for a cup of tea?вАЭ

Parker accepted the offer, and sank luxuriously into a corner of the Chesterfield. After the extraordinary discomfort of French furniture there was solace in the enervating springiness beneath him, the cushions behind his head, and WimseyвАЩs excellent cigarettes. What Bunter had meant by saying that things were вАЬnot quite straight yetвАЭ he could not divine. A leaping wood fire was merrily reflected in the spotless surface of the black baby grand; the mellow calf bindings of Lord PeterвАЩs rare editions glowed softly against the black and primrose walls; the vases were filled with tawny chrysanthemums; the latest editions of all the papers were on the tableпїњвАФas though the owner had never been absent.

Over his tea Mr.¬†Parker drew out the photographs of Lady Mary and Denis Cathcart from his breast pocket. He stood them up against the teapot and stared at them, looking from one to the other as if trying to force a meaning from their faintly smirking, self-conscious gaze. He referred again to his Paris notes, ticking off various points with a pencil. вАЬDamn!вАЭ said Mr.¬†Parker, gazing at Lady Mary. вАЬDamnпїњвАФdamnпїњвАФdamnпїњвАФвАЭ

The train of thought he was pursuing was an extraordinarily interesting one. Image after image, each rich in suggestion, crowded into his mind. Of course, one couldnвАЩt think properly in ParisпїњвАФit was so uncomfortable and the houses were central heated. Here, where so many problems had been unravelled, there was a good fire. Cathcart had been sitting before the fire. Of course, he wanted to think out a problem. When cats sat staring into the fire they were thinking out problems. It was odd he should not have thought of that before. When the green-eyed cat sat before the fire one sank right down into a sort of rich, black, velvety suggestiveness which was most important. It was luxurious to be able to think so lucidly as this, because otherwise it would be a pity to exceed the speed limitпїњвАФand the black moors were reeling by so fast. But now he had really got the formula he wouldnвАЩt forget it again. The connection was just thereпїњвАФclose, thick, richly coherent.

вАЬThe glassblowerвАЩs cat is bompstable,вАЭ said Mr.¬†Parker aloud and distinctly.

вАЬIвАЩm charmed to hear it,вАЭ replied Lord Peter, with a friendly grin. вАЬHad a good nap, old man?вАЭ

вАЬIпїњвАФwhat?вАЭ said Mr.¬†Parker. вАЬHullo! Watcher mean, nap? I had got hold of a most important train of thought, and youвАЩve put it out of my head. What was it? CatпїњвАФcatпїњвАФcatпїњвАФвАЭ He groped wildly.

вАЬYou said вАШThe glassblowerвАЩs cat is bompstable,вАЩвАКвАЭ retorted Lord Peter. вАЬItвАЩs a perfectly rippinвАЩ word, but I donвАЩt know what you mean by it.вАЭ

вАЬBompstable?вАЭ said Mr.¬†Parker, blushing slightly. вАЬBompпїњвАФoh, well, perhaps youвАЩre rightпїњвАФI may have dozed off. But, you know, I thought IвАЩd just got the clue to the whole thing. I attached the greatest importance to that phrase. Even nowпїњвАФNo, now I come to think of it, my train of thought doesnвАЩt seem quite to hold together. What a pity. I thought it was so lucid.вАЭ

вАЬNever mind,вАЭ said Lord Peter. вАЬJust back?вАЭ

вАЬCrossed last night. Any news?вАЭ

вАЬLots.вАЭ

вАЬGood?вАЭ

вАЬNo.вАЭ

ParkerвАЩs eyes wandered to the photographs.

вАЬI donвАЩt believe it,вАЭ he said obstinately. вАЬIвАЩm damned if IвАЩm going to believe a word of it.вАЭ

вАЬA word of what?вАЭ

вАЬOf whatever it is.вАЭ

вАЬYouвАЩll have to believe it, Charles, as far as it goes,вАЭ said his friend softly, filling his pipe with decided little digs of the fingers. вАЬI donвАЩt sayвАЭпїњвАФdigпїњвАФвАЬthat MaryвАЭпїњвАФdigпїњвАФвАЬshot CathcartвАЭпїњвАФdig, digпїњвАФвАЬbut she has liedвАЭпїњвАФdigпїњвАФвАЬagain and again.вАЭпїњвАФDig, digпїњвАФвАЬShe knows who did itвАЭпїњвАФdigпїњвАФвАЬshe was prepared for itвАЭпїњвАФdigпїњвАФвАЬsheвАЩs malingering and lying to keep the fellow shieldedвАЭпїњвАФdigпїњвАФвАЬand we shall have to make her speak.вАЭ Here he struck a match and lit the pipe in a series of angry little puffs.

вАЬIf you can think,вАЭ said Mr.¬†Parker, with some heat, вАЬthat that womanвАЭпїњвАФhe indicated the photographsпїњвАФвАЬhad any hand in murdering Cathcart, I donвАЩt care what your evidence is, youпїњвАФhang it all, Wimsey, sheвАЩs your own sister.вАЭ

вАЬGerald is my brother,вАЭ said Wimsey quietly. вАЬYou donвАЩt suppose IвАЩm exactly enjoying this business, do you? But I think we shall get along very much better if we try to keep our tempers.вАЭ

вАЬIвАЩm awfully sorry,вАЭ said Parker. вАЬCanвАЩt think why I said thatпїњвАФrotten bad formпїњвАФbeg pardon, old man.вАЭ

вАЬThe best thing we can do,вАЭ said Wimsey, вАЬis to look the evidence in the face, however ugly. And I donвАЩt mind admittinвАЩ that some of itвАЩs a positive gargoyle.

вАЬMy mother turned up at Riddlesdale on Friday. She marched upstairs at once and took possession of Mary, while I drooped about in the hall and teased the cat, and generally made a nuisance of myself. You know. Presently old Dr.¬†Thorpe called. I went and sat on the chest on the landing. Presently the bell rings and Ellen comes upstairs. Mother and Thorpe popped out and caught her just outside MaryвАЩs room, and they jibber-jabbered a lot, and presently mother came barging down the passage to the bathroom with her heels tapping and her earrings simply dancing with irritation. I sneaked after вАЩem to the bathroom door, but I couldnвАЩt see anything, because they were blocking the doorway, but I heard mother say, вАШThere, now, what did I tell youвАЩ; and Ellen said, вАШLawks! your grace, whoвАЩd вАЩaвАЩ thought it?вАЩ; and my mother said, вАШAll I can say is, if I had to depend on you people to save me from being murdered with arsenic or that other stuff with the name like anemones4пїњвАФyou know what I meanпїњвАФthat that very attractive-looking man with the preposterous beard used to make away with his wife and mother-in-law (who was vastly the more attractive of the two, poor thing), I might be being cut up and analyzed by Dr.¬†Spilsbury nowпїњвАФsuch a horrid, distasteful job he must have of it, poor man, and the poor little rabbits, too.вАЩвАКвАЭ Wimsey paused for breath, and Parker laughed in spite of his anxiety.

вАЬI wonвАЩt vouch for the exact words,вАЭ said Wimsey, вАЬbut it was to that effectпїњвАФyou know my motherвАЩs style. Old Thorpe tried to look dignified, but mother ruffled up like a little hen and said, looking beadily at him: вАШIn my day we called that kind of thing hysterics and naughtiness. We didnвАЩt let girls pull the wool over our eyes like that. I suppose you call it a neurosis, or a suppressed desire, or a reflex, and coddle it. You might have let that silly child make herself really ill. You are all perfectly ridiculous, and no more fit to take care of yourselves than a lot of babiesпїњвАФnot but what there are plenty of poor little things in the slums that look after whole families and show more sense than the lot of you put together. I am very angry with Mary, advertising herself in this way, and sheвАЩs not to be pitied.вАЩ You know,вАЭ said Wimsey, вАЬI think thereвАЩs often a great deal in what oneвАЩs mother says.вАЭ

вАЬI believe you,вАЭ said Parker.

вАЬWell, I got hold of mother afterwards and asked her what it was all about. She said Mary wouldnвАЩt tell her anything about herself or her illness; just asked to be let alone. Then Thorpe came along and talked about nervous shockпїњвАФsaid he couldnвАЩt understand these fits of sickness, or the way MaryвАЩs temperature hopped about. Mother listened, and told him to go and see what the temperature was now. Which he did, and in the middle mother called him away to the dressing-table. But, beinвАЩ a wily old bird, you see, she kept her eyes on the looking-glass, and nipped round just in time to catch Mary stimulatinвАЩ the thermometer to terrific leaps on the hot-water bottle.вАЭ

вАЬWell, IвАЩm damned!вАЭ said Parker.

вАЬSo was Thorpe. All mother said was, that if he wasnвАЩt too old a bird yet to be taken in by that hoary trick heвАЩd no business to be gettinвАЩ himself up as a grey-haired family practitioner. So then she asked the girl about the sick fitsпїњвАФwhen they happened, and how often, and was it after meals or before, and so on, and at last she got out of them that it generally happened a bit after breakfast and occasionally at other times. Mother said she couldnвАЩt make it out at first, because sheвАЩd hunted all over the room for bottles and things, till at last she asked who made the bed, thinkinвАЩ, you see, Mary might have hidden something under the mattress. So Ellen said she usually made it while Mary had her bath. вАШWhenвАЩs that?вАЩ says mother. вАШJust before her breakfast,вАЩ bleats the girl. вАШGod forgive you all for a set of nincompoops,вАЩ says my mother. вАШWhy didnвАЩt you say so before?вАЩ So away they all trailed to the bathroom, and there, sittinвАЩ up quietly on the bathroom shelf among the bath salts and the EllimanвАЩs embrocation and the Kruschen feelings and the toothbrushes and things, was the family bottle of ipecacuanhaпїњвАФthree-quarters empty! Mother saidпїњвАФwell, I told you what she said. By the way, how do you spell ipecacuanha?вАЭ

Mr.¬†Parker spelt it.

вАЬDamn you!вАЭ said Lord Peter. вАЬI did think IвАЩd stumped you that time. I believe you went and looked it up beforehand. No decent-minded person would know how to spell ipecacuanha out of his own head. Anyway, as you were saying, itвАЩs easy to see which side of the family has the detective instinct.вАЭ

вАЬI didnвАЩt say soпїњвАФвАЭ

вАЬI know. Why didnвАЩt you? I think my motherвАЩs talents deserve a little acknowledgment. I said so to her, as a matter of fact, and she replied in these memorable words: вАШMy dear child, you can give it a long name if you like, but IвАЩm an old-fashioned woman and I call it mother-wit, and itвАЩs so rare for a man to have it that if he does you write a book about him and call him Sherlock Holmes.вАЩ However, apart from all that, I said to mother (in private, of course), вАШItвАЩs all very well, but I canвАЩt believe that Mary has been going to all this trouble to make herself horribly sick and frighten us all just to show off. Surely she isnвАЩt that sort.вАЩ Mother looked at me as steady as an owl, and quoted a whole lot of examples of hysteria, ending up with the servant-girl who threw paraffin about all over somebodyвАЩs house to make them think it was haunted, and finished upпїњвАФthat if all these newfangled doctors went out of their way to invent subconsciousness and kleptomania, and complexes and other fancy descriptions to explain away when people had done naughty things, she thought one might just as well take advantage of the fact.вАЭ

вАЬWimsey,вАЭ said Parker, much excited, вАЬdid she mean she suspected something?вАЭ

вАЬMy dear old chap,вАЭ replied Lord Peter, вАЬwhatever can be known about Mary by putting two and two together my mother knows. I told her all we knew up to that point, and she took it all in, in her funny way, you know, never answering anything directly, and then she put her head on one side and said: вАШIf Mary had listened to me, and done something useful instead of that V.A.D. work, which never came to much, if you ask meпїњвАФnot that I have anything against V.A.D.вАЩs in a general way, but that silly woman Mary worked under was the most terrible snob on GodвАЩs earthпїњвАФand there were very much more sensible things which Mary might really have done well, only that she was so crazy to get to LondonпїњвАФI shall always say it was the fault of that ridiculous clubпїњвАФwhat could you expect of a place where you ate such horrible food, all packed into an underground cellar painted pink and talking away at the tops of their voices, and never any evening dressпїњвАФonly Soviet jumpers and side-whiskers. Anyhow, IвАЩve told that silly old man what to say about it, and theyвАЩll never be able to think of a better explanation for themselves.вАЩ Indeed, you know,вАЭ said Peter, вАЬI think if any of them start getting inquisitive, theyвАЩll have mother down on them like a ton of bricks.вАЭ

вАЬWhat do you really think yourself?вАЭ asked Parker.

вАЬI havenвАЩt come yet to the unpleasantest bit of the lot,вАЭ said Peter. вАЬIвАЩve only just heard it, and it did give me a nasty jar, IвАЩll admit. Yesterday I got a letter from Lubbock saying he would like to see me, so I trotted up here and dropped in on him this morning. You remember I sent him a stain off one of MaryвАЩs skirts which Bunter had cut out for me? I had taken a squint at it myself, and didnвАЩt like the look of it, so I sent it up to Lubbock, ex abundantia cautelae; and IвАЩm sorry to say he confirms me. ItвАЩs human blood, Charles, and IвАЩm afraid itвАЩs CathcartвАЩs.вАЭ

вАЬButпїњвАФIвАЩve lost the thread of this a bit.вАЭ

вАЬWell, the skirt must have got stained the day CathcartпїњвАФdied, as that was the last day on which the party was out on the moors, and if it had been there earlier Ellen would have cleaned it off. Afterwards Mary strenuously resisted EllenвАЩs efforts to take the skirt away, and made an amateurish effort to tidy it up herself with soap. So I think we may conclude that Mary knew the stains were there, and wanted to avoid discovery. She told Ellen that the blood was from a grouseпїњвАФwhich must have been a deliberate untruth.вАЭ

вАЬPerhaps,вАЭ said Parker, struggling against hope to make out a case for Lady Mary, вАЬshe only said, вАШOh! one of the birds must have bled,вАЩ or something like that.вАЭ

вАЬI donвАЩt believe,вАЭ said Peter, вАЬthat one could get a great patch of human blood on oneвАЩs clothes like that and not know what it was. She must have knelt right in it. It was three or four inches across.вАЭ

Parker shook his head dismally, and consoled himself by making a note.

вАЬWell, now,вАЭ went on Peter, вАЬon Wednesday night everybody comes in and dines and goes to bed except Cathcart, who rushes out and stays out. At 11:50 the gamekeeper, Hardraw, hears a shot which may very well have been fired in the clearing where theпїњвАФwell, letвАЩs say the accidentпїњвАФtook place. The time also agrees with the medical evidence about Cathcart having already been dead three or four hours when he was examined at 4:30. Very well. At 3¬†a.m. Jerry comes home from somewhere or other and finds the body. As he is bending over it, Mary arrives in the most apropos manner from the house in her coat and cap and walking shoes. Now what is her story? She says that at three oвАЩclock she was awakened by a shot. Now nobody else heard that shot, and we have the evidence of Mrs.¬†Pettigrew-Robinson, who slept in the next room to Mary, with her window open according to her immemorial custom, that she lay broad awake from 2¬†a.m. till a little after 3¬†a.m., when the alarm was given, and heard no shot. According to Mary, the shot was loud enough to waken her on the other side of the building. ItвАЩs odd, isnвАЩt it, that the person already awake should swear so positively that she heard nothing of a noise loud enough to waken a healthy young sleeper next door? And, in any case, if that was the shot that killed Cathcart, he can barely have been dead when my brother found himпїњвАФand again, in that case, how was there time for him to be carried up from the shrubbery to the conservatory?вАЭ

вАЬWeвАЩve been over all this ground,вАЭ said Parker, with an expression of distaste. вАЬWe agreed that we couldnвАЩt attach any importance to the story of the shot.вАЭ

вАЬIвАЩm afraid weвАЩve got to attach a great deal of importance to it,вАЭ said Lord Peter gravely. вАЬNow, what does Mary do? Either she thought the shotпїњвАФвАЭ

вАЬThere was no shot.вАЭ

вАЬI know that. But IвАЩm examining the discrepancies of her story. She said she did not give the alarm because she thought it was probably only poachers. But, if it was poachers, it would be absurd to go down and investigate. So she explains that she thought it might be burglars. Now how does she dress to go and look for burglars? What would you or I have done? I think we would have taken a dressing-gown, a stealthy kind of pair of slippers, and perhaps a poker or a stout stickпїњвАФnot a pair of walking shoes, a coat, and a cap, of all things!вАЭ

вАЬIt was a wet night,вАЭ mumbled Parker.

вАЬMy dear chap, if itвАЩs burglars youвАЩre looking for you donвАЩt expect to go and hunt them round the garden. Your first thought is that theyвАЩre getting into the house, and your idea is to slip down quietly and survey them from the staircase or behind the dining-room door. Anyhow, fancy a present-day girl, who rushes about bareheaded in all weathers, stopping to embellish herself in a cap for a burglar-huntпїњвАФdamn it all, Charles, it wonвАЩt wash, you know! And she walks straight off to the conservatory and comes upon the corpse, exactly as if she knew where to look for it beforehand.вАЭ

Parker shook his head again.

вАЬWell, now. She sees Gerald stooping over CathcartвАЩs body. What does she say? Does she ask whatвАЩs the matter? Does she ask who it is? She exclaims: вАШO God! Gerald, youвАЩve killed him,вАЩ and then she says, as if on second thoughts, вАШOh, itвАЩs Denis! What has happened? Has there been an accident?вАЩ Now, does that strike you as natural?вАЭ

вАЬNo. But it rather suggests to me that it wasnвАЩt Cathcart she expected to see there, but somebody else.вАЭ

вАЬDoes it? It rather sounds to me as if she was pretending not to know who it was. First she says, вАШYouвАЩve killed him!вАЩ and then, recollecting that she isnвАЩt supposed to know who вАШheвАЩ is, she says, вАШWhy, itвАЩs Denis!вАЩвАКвАЭ

вАЬIn any case, then, if her first exclamation was genuine, she didnвАЩt expect to find the man dead.вАЭ

вАЬNoпїњвАФnoпїњвАФwe must remember that. The death was a surprise. Very well. Then Gerald sends Mary up for help. And hereвАЩs where a little bit of evidence comes in that you picked up and sent along. Do you remember what Mrs.¬†Pettigrew-Robinson said to you in the train?вАЭ

вАЬAbout the door slamming on the landing, do you mean?вАЭ

вАЬYes. Now IвАЩll tell you something that happened to me the other morning. I was burstinвАЩ out of the bathroom in my usual breezy way when I caught myself a hell of a whack on that old chest on the landinвАЩ, and the lid lifted up and shut down, plonk! That gave me an idea, and I thought IвАЩd have a squint inside. IвАЩd got the lid up and was lookinвАЩ at some sheets and stuff that were folded up at the bottom, when I heard a sort of gasp, and there was Mary, starinвАЩ at me, as white as a ghost. She gave me a turn, by Jove, but nothinвАЩ like the turn IвАЩd given her. Well, she wouldnвАЩt say anything to me, and got hysterical, and I hauled her back to her room. But IвАЩd seen something on those sheets.вАЭ

вАЬWhat?вАЭ

вАЬSilver sand.вАЭ

вАЬSilverпїњвАФвАЭ

вАЬDвАЩyou remember those cacti in the greenhouse, and the place where somebodyвАЩd put a suitcase or something down?вАЭ

вАЬYes.вАЭ

вАЬWell, there was a lot of silver sand scattered aboutпїњвАФthe sort people stick round some kinds of bulbs and things.вАЭ

вАЬAnd that was inside the chest too?вАЭ

вАЬYes. Wait a moment. After the noise Mrs.¬†Pettigrew-Robinson heard, Mary woke up Freddy and then the Pettigrew-RobinsonsпїњвАФand then what?вАЭ

вАЬShe locked herself into her room.вАЭ

вАЬYes. And shortly afterwards she came down and joined the others in the conservatory, and it was at this point everybody remembered noticing that she was wearing a cap and coat and walking shoes over pajamas and bare feet.вАЭ

вАЬYou are suggesting,вАЭ said Parker, вАЬthat Lady Mary was already awake and dressed at three oвАЩclock, that she went out by the conservatory door with her suitcase, expecting to meet theпїњвАФthe murderer of herпїњвАФdamn it, Wimsey!вАЭ

вАЬWe neednвАЩt go so far as that,вАЭ said Peter; вАЬwe decided that she didnвАЩt expect to find Cathcart dead.вАЭ

вАЬNo. Well, she went, presumably to meet somebody.вАЭ

вАЬShall we say, pro tem., she went to meet No.¬†10?вАЭ suggested Wimsey softly.

вАЬI suppose we may as well say so. When she turned on the torch and saw the Duke stooping over Cathcart she thoughtпїњвАФby Jove, Wimsey, I was right after all! When she said, вАШYouвАЩve killed him!вАЩ she meant No.¬†10пїњвАФshe thought it was No.¬†10вАЩs body.вАЭ

вАЬOf course!вАЭ cried Wimsey. вАЬIвАЩm a fool! Yes. Then she said, вАШItвАЩs DenisпїњвАФwhat has happened?вАЩ ThatвАЩs quite clear. And, meanwhile, what did she do with the suitcase?вАЭ

вАЬI see it all now,вАЭ cried Parker. вАЬWhen she saw that the body wasnвАЩt the body of No.¬†10 she realized that No.¬†10 must be the murderer. So her game was to prevent anybody knowing that No.¬†10 had been there. So she shoved the suitcase behind the cacti. Then, when she went upstairs, she pulled it out again, and hid it in the oak chest on the landing. She couldnвАЩt take it to her room, of course, because if anybodyвАЩd heard her come upstairs it would seem odd that she should run to her room before calling the others. Then she knocked up Arbuthnot and the Pettigrew-RobinsonsпїњвАФsheвАЩd be in the dark, and theyвАЩd be flustered and wouldnвАЩt see exactly what she had on. Then she escaped from Mrs.¬†P., ran into her room, took off the skirt in which she had knelt by CathcartвАЩs side, and the rest of her clothes, and put on her pajamas and the cap, which someone might have noticed, and the coat, which they must have noticed, and the shoes, which had probably left footmarks already. Then she could go down and show herself. Meantime sheвАЩd concocted the burglar story for the CoronerвАЩs benefit.вАЭ

вАЬThatвАЩs about it,вАЭ said Peter. вАЬI suppose she was so desperately anxious to throw us off the scent of No.¬†10 that it never occurred to her that her story was going to help implicate her brother.вАЭ

вАЬShe realized it at the inquest,вАЭ said Parker eagerly. вАЬDonвАЩt you remember how hastily she grasped at the suicide theory?вАЭ

вАЬAnd when she found that she was simply saving herпїњвАФwell, No.¬†10пїњвАФin order to hang her brother, she lost her head, took to her bed, and refused to give any evidence at all. Seems to me thereвАЩs an extra allowance of fools in my family,вАЭ said Peter gloomily.

вАЬWell, what could she have done, poor girl?вАЭ asked Parker. He had been growing almost cheerful again. вАЬAnyway, sheвАЩs clearedпїњвАФвАЭ

вАЬAfter a fashion,вАЭ said Peter, вАЬbut weвАЩre not out of the wood yet by a long way. Why is she hand-in-glove with No.¬†10 who is at least a blackmailer if not a murderer? How did GeraldвАЩs revolver come on the scene? And the green-eyed cat? How much did Mary know of that meeting between No.¬†10 and Denis Cathcart? And if she was seeing and meeting the man she might have put the revolver into his hands any time.вАЭ

вАЬNo, no,вАЭ said Parker. вАЬWimsey, donвАЩt think such ugly things as that.вАЭ

вАЬHell!вАЭ cried Peter, exploding. вАЬIвАЩll have the truth of this beastly business if we all go to the gallows together!вАЭ

At this moment Bunter entered with a telegram addressed to Wimsey. Lord Peter read as follows:

вАЬParty traced London; seen Marylebone Friday. Further information from Scotland Yard.пїњвАФPolice-Superintendent Gosling, Ripley.вАЭ

вАЬGood egg!вАЭ cried Wimsey. вАЬNow weвАЩre gettinвАЩ down to it. Stay here, thereвАЩs a good man, in case anything turns up. IвАЩll run round to the Yard now. TheyвАЩll send you up dinner, and tell Bunter to give you a bottle of the Ch√Ґteau YquemпїњвАФitвАЩs rather decent. So long.вАЭ
